coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction through improved lattice oxygen activity via carbon dots incorporation into MOFs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Chen, Delun

Wang, Bingrong
Yu, Rentong
coredata.dc:description: Emerging of the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) provides a new opportunity for enhancing oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity. However, its stability suffers from metal cation dissolution and lattice oxygen anionic redox chemistry. In this paper, carbon dots (CDs)-modified nickel–iron MOF (Metal-Organic Framework) nanosheets (NiFe-BDC/CDs) were prepared for efficient OER electrocatalysis. The introduction of CDs promotes the hybridization of the O 2p band in the MOF with the metal 3d band near the Fermi level, leading to improved involvement of lattice oxygen in the oxygen evolution reaction. Additionally, C-M bonds formed between CDs and metal sites in MOF enhanced the stability of electrocatalyst. As results, the prepared NiFe-BDC/CDs electrodes demonstrated a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at overpotentials of 235 and 250 mV in alkaline freshwater and alkaline seawater, respectively, and a remarkable stability in alkaline seawater at 500 mA cm−2 for >100 h. This study provides a simple and versatile strategy for the design of OER electrocatalysts with highly active transition metal-based MOFs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Carbon dots
Lattice oxygen
Seawater electrolysis
Oxygen evolution reaction

KLET-202209).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis processes of NiFe-BDC/CDs; (b) The XRD patterns; (c) TEM of NiFe-BDC/CDs; (d) HRTEM of NiFe-BDC/CDs; (e) CDs particle size distribution; (f) TEM and corresponding element mapping images of NiFe-BDC/CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) Ni 2p, and (d) Fe 2p of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs.

Electrocatalytic performance of the all samples in 1.0 M KOH with Seawater solution. (a) LSV polarization curves; (b) Tafel plots; (c) fitted EIS plots with an inset of the equivalent circuit; (d) Capacitive currents as a function of scan rates; (e) The ECSA-normalized LSV curves; (f) i-t curves of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs.

Mechanism analysis of OER pathway. LSV plots of (a) NiFe-BDC/BDC and (b) NiFe-BDC in electrolytes with different pH conditions; (c) the relationship between the logarithm of current density of NiFe-BDC/BDC and NiFe-BDC; LSV curves of (d) NiFe-BDC/CDs and (e) NiFe-BDC in 1.0 M KOH and 1.0 M TMAOH; (f) Raman spectra of NiFe-BDC/CDs nanosheets after running at 1.55 V vs. RHE for 60 min in 1.0 M TMAOH and 1.0 M KOH solutions.

(a) In situ Raman spectra of NiFe-BDC/CDs in 1.0 M KOH with Seawater solution; The XPS high-resolution spectra of (b) Ni 2p, (c) Fe 2p in NiFe-BDC/CDs after OER; (d–f) TEM image, (g) HRTEM image and (h) EDS mapping images of NiFe-BDC/CDs after OER.

(a) Structure model of nife-bdc/cds; (b) difference charge density of nife-bdc/cds, blue represents a decrease in charge density, while yellow represents the accumulation of charge density; (c) Gibbs free energy maps of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs catalysts at Fe sites during OER processes in AEM and LOM pathways; (d) Gibbs free energy diagrams of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs catalysts at Ni sites during OER processes in AEM and LOM pathways; (e) Partial density of states (PDOS) of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs; (f) Vacancy formation energies at the Fe sites of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs; (g) Gibbs free energy diagrams of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs catalysts at Fe sites during ClER processes; (h) Adsorption energy of OH− and Cl− on the Fe site of NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs samples.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction through improved lattice oxygen activity via carbon dots incorporation into MOFs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea, Hainan University, Haikou, Hainan 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea, Hainan University, Haikou, Hainan 570228, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Laboratory of Child Cognition & Behavior Development of Hainan Province, Qiongtai Normal University, Haikou 571127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Child Cognition & Behavior Development of Hainan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Laboratory of Child Cognition & Behavior Development of Hainan Province, Qiongtai Normal University, Haikou 571127, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward one-pot solvothermal approach was utilized to synthesize the NiFe-BDC/CDs electrocatalyst, characterized by a lattice oxygen reaction pathway. By incorporating carbon dots (CDs), there is an overlapping hybridization between the O 2p band and the metal 3d band in the MOF (metal–organic framework) near the Fermi level, facilitating the participation of lattice oxygen in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and consequently improving OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs. Furthermore, the integrated CDs can form bonds with metal sites within NiFe-BDC, elevating the vacancy formation energy of iron sites. This not only enhances the adsorption energy of Cl− but also reduces the adsorption energy of OH−, thereby effectively mitigating corrosion and preserving the active sites.

Emerging of the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) provides a new opportunity for enhancing oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity. However, its stability suffers from metal cation dissolution and lattice oxygen anionic redox chemistry. In this paper, carbon dots (CDs)-modified nickel–iron MOF (Metal-Organic Framework) nanosheets (NiFe-BDC/CDs) were prepared for efficient OER electrocatalysis. The introduction of CDs promotes the hybridization of the O 2p band in the MOF with the metal 3d band near the Fermi level, leading to improved involvement of lattice oxygen in the oxygen evolution reaction. Additionally, C-M bonds formed between CDs and metal sites in MOF enhanced the stability of electrocatalyst. As results, the prepared NiFe-BDC/CDs electrodes demonstrated a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at overpotentials of 235 and 250 mV in alkaline freshwater and alkaline seawater, respectively, and a remarkable stability in alkaline seawater at 500 mA cm−2 for >100 h. This study provides a simple and versatile strategy for the design of OER electrocatalysts with highly active transition metal-based MOFs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen energy, as a clean, non-polluting and high energy density energy source, holds broad application prospects in replacing non-renewable energy sources [1–5]. Electrolysis of seawater offers a promising solution to address freshwater scarcity, as it utilizes the most abundant natural resource on the earth [6–8]. However, the presence of chloride ions poses a significantly challenge. Chloride oxidation competes with oxygen evolution reaction (OER), and Cl− etching can cause the electrodes to collapse [9]. To enable efficient seawater electrolysis, it is crucial to develop electrocatalysts that can both improve the catalytic activity of OER and resist the corrosive ability of Cl−. In particular, OER electrocatalysts should operate with an overpotential below 490 mV in alkaline condition to avoid possible competition with Cl− oxidation and ensure uniform OER selectivity [10,11].

The lattice oxygen oxidation mechanism (LOM) is able to overcome the limitations associated with the conventional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) by enabling direct OO coupling to produce O2, thus lowering the OER overpotential, accelerating OER kinetics, and strengthening the competitive ability against the chloride oxidation reaction [12–14]. However, lattice oxygen activation is required to enable the LOM pathway. Generally, adjusting the hybridization near the Fermi level between the O 2p band and the metal 3d (M-3d) band can increase the covalency of the metal–oxygen (M−O) bond, facilitating the involvement of lattice oxygen in the OER [15–17]. While previous research has focused on increasing the concentration of oxygen vacancies to activate lattice oxygen for the reaction, which can lead to structural changes and reduced stability as many oxygen vacancies (VO) are formed dynamically during the LOM process [18–21]. Furthermore, many LOM-based electrocatalysts, such as oxides and oxyhydroxides, often suffer from poor conductivity and stability, severely hindering further enhancement of their activity [22–24]. Therefore, it is highly necessary to develop OER electrocatalysts with improved conductivity and stability that align with the LOM pathway. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as effective OER catalysts due to their high specific surface area, remarkable structural complexity, and tunability of metal cation multi-coordination chemistry [25–29]. Incorporating conductive carbon materials, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and carbon dots (CDs) is recognized as an important strategy to improve electrochemical activity and stability [30–32]. Among them, CDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with lateral sizes ranging from 2.0 to 10 nm, combining the characteristics of organic polymers, bulk carbon, and quantum dots. They exhibit good conductivity, serving as both electron acceptors and donors [33]. And their electron transfer characteristics provide excellent interfacial stability [34,35]. In view of this, it is foreseeable that coupling MOFs with CDs will simultaneously enhance charge transfer capabilities and expose high-activity metal sites, thereby improving the OER performance of MOFs.

Based on the above considerations, a novel OER electrocatalyst, carbon dot-modified nickel–iron MOF electrocatalyst (NiFe-BDC/CDs), based on the LOM pathway, was synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. Experimental data and theoretical calculations indicate that the introduction of CDs with the formed CM bonds enhanced the hybridization between the M 3d band and the O 2p band near the Fermi level, effectively increasing the participation of lattice oxygen in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), thereby accelerating OER kinetics and improving the OER activity and stability of the electrocatalyst. Therefore, the NiFe-BDC/CDs electrode achieved a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at overpotentials of 235 and 250 mV in alkaline freshwater and alkaline seawater, respectively, and demonstrated a long stability in alkaline seawater at 500 mA cm−2 for continuous operation exceeding 100 h (h).

NiFe-BDC/CDs were prepared via a facile one-pot solvothermal method as shown in Fig. 1 a. The corresponding X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1b) indicate that the formed NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs have similar diffraction peaks to the simulated Ni-BDC, with the main peaks located at 8.9° on the (2 0 0) face of the MOF structure [36]. The difference lies in the partial substitution of Ni with Fe to form NiFe-BDC, resulting in a change in the type and ratio of metal ions. Consequently, compared to the simulated Ni-BDC, the main peaks at 8.9° for NiFe-BDC and NiFe-BDC/CDs undergo slight shifts [37], indicating that the introduction of CDs does not significantly alter the MOF’s crystal structure. In addition, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. S1) reveals enhanced signals of almost all carbon-containing functional groups (e.g., COO, CO, CH) [38,39] after the introduction of CDs, which verified the presence of surface organic ligands in the CDs, confirming the successful introduction of CDs into NiFe-BDC [40]. The morphology of NiFe-BDC/CDs was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It is evident that NiFe-BDC are dispersed arrays of nanosheets (Fig. S2a, b). Fig. S2c and d demonstrates that the morphology of NiFe-BDC did not change after the addition of CDs, with its nanosheet arrays well retained. The nanosheet morphology of NiFe-BDC/CDs was also further verified by TEM in Fig. S3, where the CDs were uniformly distributed in the nanosheets (Fig. 1c). In the High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1d) a lattice spacing of 0.210 nm is observed, which corresponds to the (100) face of the carbon, as well as 0.198 nm and 0.223 nm, which correspond to the (203) and (112) faces of the BDC, respectively. The particle size distribution of CDs was calculated from TEM images, which was around 3.680 nm (Fig. 1e). The corresponding EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 1f) clearly show that the elements C, O, Fe, and Ni are uniformly distributed in the NiFe-BDC/CDs, further confirming the successful introduction of CDs into the MOF structure.

In order to further investigate the electronic coupling between CDs and NiFe-BDC, the effect of CDs doping on the electronic configuration of NiFe-BDC was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra of NiFe-BDC/CDs nanosheets revealed the corresponding elemental coexistence in Fig. S4, which agrees with the results of the EDS elemental mapping diagram (Fig. 1f). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2 a shows a remarkable increase in the intensity of the CC sp2 peak at 284.4 eV in NiFe-BDC/CDs after the introduction of CDs. This increase is due to the formation of C-M bonds following the CDs incorporation [41], as the bonding between CDs and the metal sites of the MOF upon the incorporation of CDs reinforces the structural stability of the MOF. In addition, the introduction of CDs lead to a 0.15 eV shift towards higher binding energies of the overall oxygen binding energy, weakening the Coulombic force between the metal cation and the oxygen anion [42]. This effect may result in higher lattice oxygen activity (Fig. 2b).

Ni 2p (Fig. 2c) of NiFe-BDC/CDs can be classified as a double peak with two satellite peaks (861.60 and 879.95 eV), Ni2+ (855.97 and 873.47 eV) and Ni3+ (856.88 and 875.29 eV) [43]. Fe 2p (Fig. 2d) shows a satellite peak at 715.78 eV and double peaks for Fe2+ (710.10 and 723.22 eV) and Fe3+ (713.02 and 725.82 eV) [44]. The binding energies of Ni 2p in NiFe-BDC/CDs were slightly shifted to higher energies by 0.12 eV compared to the overall Ni 2p in NiFe-BDCs, while the overall binding energy of Fe 2p was negatively shifted by 0.1 eV. These changes in binding energies suggest that the incorporation of the CDs led to interactions with the electronic structure in NiFe-BDC. This indicates theformationof new Ni/FeOC bonds within the catalyst, facilitating electron transfer between the C atoms in CDs and the metal atoms Ni/Fe of the MOF. [45,46] Consequently, this optimized electronic structure enhances the charge transport of the compound, thereby improving the OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs. [46].

The OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs in 1.0 M KOH、1.0 M KOH with 0.5 M NaCl and 1.0 M KOH with Sea water electrolyte was measured using a standard three-electrode system. Firstly, the OER activity of NiFe-BDC synthesized with different Ni and Fe molar ratios was investigated and the results obtained are shown in Fig. S5a, therefore the optimal Ni:Fe molar ratio was determined to be 2:1. Other optimal synthesis conditions were determined in Fig. S5b and c, with a reaction time of 12 h and 5 mg of CDs introduction. For the purpose of comparing electrochemical properties, we additionally crafted a standard NiFe-LDH sample. The successful synthesis of NiFe-LDH was validated through XRD analysis (Fig. S6), while its morphology, captured in SEM images (Fig. S7), exhibited a resemblance to the flake-like structure of NiFe-BDC/CDs. Fig. S8a shows the linear scanning voltammetry curves of these electrocatalysts, where NiFe-BDC/CDs reached a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at an overpotential of only 235 mV, which is lower than that of all samples, also lower than the commercial benchmark catalyst IrO2 and the standard sample FeNi-LDH (Fig. S8b and Table S1), demonstrating superior performance. Furthermore, NiFe-BDC/CDs (22.2 mV dec−1) exhibits a lower Tafel slope compared to NiFe-BDC (38.8 mV dec−1), IrO2 (53.8 mV dec−1), NiFe-LDH (55.1 mV dec−1) and CDs (58.1 mV dec−1) (Fig. S8c), indicating faster OER reaction kinetics within a certain potential range. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements further confirmed the improved charge transfer capacity of NiFe-BDC/CDs. The fitted Nyquist plots and the corresponding equivalent circuits are shown in Fig. S9a. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of NiFe-BDC/CDs is 0.2 Ω, significantly lower than that of NiFe-BDC (0.5 Ω) and CDs (3.2 Ω) (Table S2), indicating that the charge transfer potential barrier is the lowest in the OER process. The cyclic voltammetry-measured double layer capacitance (Cdl) serves as a crucial parameter for assessing the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of catalysts, as shown in Fig. S9b and Fig. S10a–c, the Cdl value of NiFe-BDC/CDs was as high as 2.52 mF cm−2, which was higher than that of NiFe-BDC (2.22 mF cm−2) and CDs (1.41 mF cm−2). The results showed that among the three catalysts, NiFe-BDC/CDs have the most potential active sites. In addition, the ECSA-normalized LSV curves of NiFe-BDC/CDs electrodes also showed better intrinsic energy spectra than those of NiFe-BDC (Fig. S9c). These findings together confirm the significant enhancement of high intrinsic OER activity by coupling CDs with NiFe-BDC. Long-term durability is another important descriptor for evaluating OER activity. The NiFe-BDC/CDs maintained a stable current density of 100 mA cm−2 for up to 24 h with negligible attenuation. (Fig. S9d).

We further investigated the electrocatalytic OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs in simulated seawater (1.0 M KOH with 0.5 M NaCl) and real seawater. In simulated seawater, only 246 mV overpotential was required at 100 mA cm−2 (Fig. S11a). The Tafel slope of the NiFe-BDC/CDs catalysts (23.3 mV dec−1) was significantly lower than those of NiFe-BDC and CDs (42.4 and 66.3 mV dec−1), as illustrated in Fig. S11b, indicating that the NiFe-BDC/CDs catalysts maintained strong OER catalytic kinetics in 1.0 M KOH with 0.5 M NaCl. The corresponding electrochemical impedance spectra of the fitted Nyquist plots and the equivalent circuits presented in Fig. S11c, along with the combined electrochemical impedance spectra and the detailed data of the EIS in Table S3 clearly show that the NiFe-BDC/CDs have the lowest charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of 0.3 Ω among all the prepare materials, while its C dl value is the highest, reaching 2.42 mF cm−2 (Figs. S11d and S12a-c). Furthermore, when normalized by ECSA, the LSV curves of NiFe-BDC/CDs electrodes in alkaline simulated seawater exhibited superior intrinsic energy characteristics compared to those of NiFe-BDC (Fig. S11e). This further proves the excellent OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs electrode in electrolysis of simulated seawater. NiFe-BDC/CDs requires overpotentials of 235 mV in freshwater and 246 mV in 1.0 M KOH with 0.5 M NaCl in OER, respectively, both of which are lower than the theoretical maximum value of about 490 mV to prevent hypochlorite formation [47]. Therefore, NiFe-BDC/CDs hold potent application prospect as a suitable OER electrocatalyst for seawater electrolysis.

The catalytic performance of all the prepared samples was evaluated in alkaline seawater solution (1.0 M KOH with seawater), with the results displayed in Fig. 3 a. The NiFe-BDC/CDs again demonstrated the best, OER activity, achieving a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at a mere potential of 250 mV. This finding highlights the superior OER performance of NiFe-BDC/CDs over other comparative materials in seawater and simulated seawater media (Table S4). In addition, the OER kinetics in alkaline seawater was evaluated using the corresponding Tafel slopes and EIS in Fig. 3b and c. NiFe-BDC/CDs consistently exhibited the lowest Tafel slope (26.6 mV dec−1) and Rct (0.5 Ω), outperforming NiFe-BDC (40.3 mV dec−1) and CDs (93.3 mV dec−1) (Fig. 3b). Similarly, its electrochemical impedance (EIS) was also lower compared to NiFe-BDC (1.2 Ω) and CDs (16.5 Ω) (Fig. 3c), as well as the highest C dl value (2.38 mF cm−2) compared to NiFe-BDC (1.65 mF cm−2) and CDs (1.42 mF cm−2) (Fig. 3d and Fig. S13a–c). Furthermore, the ECSA-normalized LSV curves of NiFe-BDC/CDs electrodes also showed better intrinsic energy spectra than those of NiFe-BDC and CDs (Fig. 3e). This suggests that the introduction of CDs enhances its activity under the harsh conditions of real seawater, while also ensures its long-term stability, as evidenced by the sustained performance over 100 h at a current density of 500 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3f). In contrast, the NiFe-BDC composite without the addition of CDs showed a significant potential increase after 23 h of continuous testing at 500 mA cm−2. NiFe-BDC/CDs exhibits remarkable stability, maintaining good performance during continuous testing at a high current density of 500 mA cm−2 for over 100 h.

The catalytic activity in simulated seawater and natural seawater was slightly lower compared to that in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. S14), which may be due to the potential poisoning effect on the electrode surface caused by Cl− ions and other ions or particles in seawater. However, the NiFe-BDC/CDs electrode showed an overpotential of 250 mV at 100 mA cm−2 in alkaline seawater, which is significantly lower than the theoretical voltage for the chloride precipitation reaction. As a result, the electrode exhibited minimal susceptibility to Cl−, thereby extending their service life. The NiFe-BDC/CDs catalyst exhibited a Faraday efficiency of 99.1 % when measured at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 (Fig. S15). Additionally, to assess the potential for chlorine oxidation during extended OER testing in alkaline seawater, N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) was employed as a hypochlorite indicator. DPD reacts with hypochlorite species to produce a pink dye with strong absorption in UV–visible spectroscopy (Figs. S16 and S17) [48,49]. The outcomes presented in Figs. S16 and S17 reveal that upon adding DPD to the solution post a 100-hour OER test, in the absence of hypochlorite, no color alteration was observed, implying the absence of hypochlorite formation [50]. This confirms the selective behavior of the NiFe-BDC/CDs catalyst towards OER during the electrolysis of alkaline seawater. These observations underscore the remarkable long-term stability and efficiency of NiFe-BDC/CDs as an OER electrocatalyst in alkaline seawater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of enhanced OER activity
ce:section.ce:para: In order to gain a comprehensive understanding of the effect of introduced carbon sites on OER activity, we further investigated the OER pathway in different samples. The conventional AEM pathway involves a four-step proton-electron co-transfer process, whereas the LOM pathway represents a non-cooperative proton-electron transfer process and exhibits pH-dependent activity [51]. Therefore, the OER properties of NiFe-BDC/CDs and NiFe-BDC were evaluated at different pH values (Fig. 4 a and b). All samples showed increased OER activity with increasing pH, but NiFe-BDC/CDs showed the most pronounced enhancement, suggesting a higher pH-dependence, as evidenced in Fig. 4c, where NiFe-BDC/CDs exhibited the largest slope between logj and pH values. This observation is consistent with the LOM pathway rather than the AEM pathway, where lattice oxygen may directly involves in the OER process [13]. Further evidence supporting the LOM pathway was obtained through the detection of negatively charged oxygen-containing species (*O2 2–), which are characteristic products of the LOM process [52]. Tetramethylammonium ion (TMA+) was introduced as a detector of O2 2–, as it traps O2 2– for strong binding and hinders OER kinetics if the LOM pathway is active [53,54]. After replacing 1.0 M KOH with 1.0 M TMAOH, the OER activity of NiFe-BDC/CDs was significantly reduced, with a higher Tafel slope from 22.2 to 54.7 mV dec−1 and an increased overpotential from 235 to 272 mV at 100 mA cm− 2 (Fig. 4d, e and Fig. S18a, b). This suggests that NiFe-BDC/CDs produce a large number of O2 2– during the OER process and follow the LOM pathway. The OER activity of NiFe-BDC also exhibited a slight decrease, suggesting some involvement of lattice oxygen in the OER reaction. Furthermore, to demonstrate the interaction between TMA+ and O2 2–, NiFe-BDC/CDs nanosheets were subjected to 1.55 V vs RHE action in 1.0 M KOH and 1.0 M TMAOH for 60 min, followed by cleaning and Raman spectroscopy tests. In the case of 1.0 M TMAOH, the corresponding Raman spectra showed two new peaks at 751 and 951 cm−1 (Fig. 4f), which corresponded to the characteristic peaks of TMA+ [55], thus confirming the interaction between TMA+ and O2 2–. Thus, we can conclude that the after the introduction of carbon dots, the lattice oxygen activity of the NiFe-BDC/CDs significantly improved, although some lattice oxygen activity was already observed on NiFe-BDC.
In order to gain further understanding of the excellent catalytic activity and stability of NiFe-BDC/CDs, real-time in situ Raman-coupled electrochemistry, XPS, HRTEM and SEM analyses were conducted.

The Raman spectroscopy in Fig. 5 a revealed a significant surface transformation during the OER process. The characteristic peaks belonging to NiFe-BDC disappeared gradually with the increase of the potential. The peaks of pristine Raman are shown in Fig. S19, the peak at 1420 cm−1, 1134 cm−1, 1608 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching mode of the CO in the carboxyl group, a vibration between the benzene ring carbon and the carboxyl group carbon, and benzene ring, respectively. While the stretching modes of CH are 860 cm−1 and 630 cm−1 [39]. These results indicate a rapid and deep phase transition. In addition, the characteristic peaks of the MOOH phase are located at 471 and 552 cm−1, respectively [56]. The proportion of metal (oxygen) hydroxide peaks measured at the initial position changed significantly at voltages above 1.4 V, which implies that part of Ni2+ was oxidized to Ni3+ to form Ni-OOH. Note that the MOOH species here are known intermediates in the AEM process, they are not the primary active sites. While the true active species is the pristine electrocatalyst, which aligns with previous reports [57–59]. However, the MOOH reconstructed on the surface of our NiFe-BDC/CDs serves as a protective layer for the electrode, mitigating the corrosion effect of chloride ions on the electrode, and thus enhancing the long-term stability of the catalyst in seawater electrolysis. Recently, research has reported that Fe/NiOOH can act as a corrosion-resistant protective layer for catalysts, enhancing their ability to resist corrosion [47,57,60].

XPS tests after OER presented the spectra of C 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p and Ni 2p intact (Fig. S20), revealing an increase in the surface concentration of Ni3+ ions and Fe3+ ions. Generally, the increase in metal ions with higher valence states tends to induce a downward shift of the metal d-band, allowing it to penetrate the p-band of the oxygen ligands. This is favorable for enhancing the involvement of lattice oxygen in the OER [61,62]. TEM (Fig. 5d–f) and SEM (Fig. S21) images show that after the nanosheet morphology of the NiFe-BDC/CDs remained intact after the OER process. The lattice stripe spacings in the HRTEM image (Fig. 5g), which are 0.142 nm, 0.243 nm, and 0.194 nm, respectively, correspond to the (110), (041), and (002) lattice planes of NiOOH (JCPDS No. 06-0141) and FeOOH (JCPDS No. 73-2326) [63,64]. This is further confirmed by the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S22). This discovery further supports the formation of MOOH species, aligning with the results obtained from in-situ Raman spectroscopy. It is obvious in the TEM image that the post-reaction CDs are well distributed on the nanosheets, and the lattice stripe spacing of 0.210 nm can also be seen in the HRTEM (Fig. 5g), which corresponds to the (100) face of carbon. The corresponding EDS elemental mappings demonstrate the coexistence and uniform dispersion of C, O, Fe and Ni elements. These results fully demonstrate the excellent structural integrity and stability of NiFe-BDC/CDs during the reaction. The NiFe-BDC/CDs catalyst can undergo reconstruction in seawater electrolysis, forming MOOH on its surface which serves as a protective layer for the electrode, thereby prolonging its service life during the process of seawater electrolysis. Moreover, the strong interaction between CDs and the NiFe-BDC framework, as demonstrated by the persistent presence of CDs after the OER reaction, are likely contributing factors to the enhanced stability.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to further elucidate the OER pathway and the impact of CDs on the catalytic properties of NiFe-BDC/CDs nanosheets, as presented in Figs. 6a and S23. The charge density difference illustrated in Fig. 6 b. highlights the accumulation of charges at the edge where NiFe-BDC interfaces with CDs, indicating the charge transfer between CDs and the metallic Ni and Fe sites. This charge transfer modulates the electronic structure of the metal sites and enhances the coupling between metal sites and oxygen, ultimately accelerating the OER kinetics. To determine whether the catalyst follows the AEM or LOM mechanism, we calculated the Gibbs free energy diagram at 0 V. The conversion of *O to *OOH is the rate-determining step (RDS) in the AEM pathway, as shown in Fig. 6c, the NiFe-BDC catalyst with Fe as the main active site has an energy barrier of 1.77 eV at this step, whereas in the LOM pathway, the energy barrier for the RDS is determined to be 1.55 eV. After the introduction of CDs, the energy barrier of RDS in AEM was determined to be 1.73 eV, while that of RDS in LOM pathway was 1.46 eV, which was smaller and closer to the experimental overpotential value, indicating that the LOM pathway was more favorable for NiFe-BDC/CDs. Subsequent analysis of the active sites demonstrated that the RDS barriers of Fe sites (1.46 eV) was smaller than those of Ni sites (1.63 eV) (Fig. 6d), confirming Fe sites as the main active sites. Moreover, the introduction of CDs further reduced the RDS energy barrier of the LOM pathway, highlighting the enhanced lattice oxygen activity of NiFe-BDC and increased involvement of lattice oxygen in the OER process. Furthermore, partial density of states (PDOS) analysis in Fig. 6e demonstrates that the introduction of CDs shifts the O 2p band closer to the Fermi level and enhances the hybridization between Fe 3d and O 2p to activate lattice oxygen, thereby generating favorable LOM [16,17,52]. Meanwhile, we have also calculated the vacancy formation energy of the Fe sites in the sample. As shown in Fig. 6f, compared to NiFe-BDC, the Fe sites of NiFe-BDC/CDs exhibit higher vacancy formation energy, which contributes to its long-term stability during the OER process.

In view of the excellent performance of the catalyst in seawater, we analyzed the Gibbs free energy changes of the catalyst in the seawater chlorine precipitation reaction (ClER) process (Fig. 6g). The results showed that the Gibbs free energy of the ClER intermediate increased considerably after the introduction of CDs, and the RDS energy barrier was much larger than that of OER, indicating that the ClER reaction could not compete with the OER reaction during the seawater electrolysis. We also compared the adsorption capacities of the catalysts for OH– and Cl− In solution, as shown in Fig. 6h. The lower adsorption energy of the NiFe-BDC /CDs electrodes for OH– on Fe sites compared to Cl− indicates a stronger affinity for OH– in seawater, making the catalyst highly favorable for the OER process.

This work demonstrated the successful development of NiFe-BDC/CDs nanosheets as an efficient OER electrocatalyst. The systematic experimental results and theoretical analyses confirm that, according to the LOM reaction mechanism, the introduction of CDs not only excites more lattice oxygen to participate in the OER process, but also improves the stability of MOF. DFT calculations confirm that CDs introduction effectively lowers the energy barriers for OER, promoting the LOM pathway for the NiFe-BDC/CDs process. Simultaneously, the formation of C-M bonds between CDs and metal sites enhances the material’s stability. NiFe-BDC/CDs electrode has excellent OER activity in alkaline freshwater and seawater, with current densities up to 100 mA cm−2 at overpotentials of 235 and 250 mV, respectively. And its remarkable stability in alkaline seawater was validated by continuous operation over 100 h at 500 mA cm−2. This study provides a new strategy for designing efficient and stable alkaline OER electrocatalysts based on MOFs, broadening the application of MOF materials in catalysis.

Zhun Tang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Delun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Weiwei Li: Investigation, Data curation. Hai Li: Investigation, Data curation. Jinchun Tu: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Xiaolin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Bingrong Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rentong Yu: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

KLET-202209).
